Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty intense but scattered. I'd dedicate around 28 hours a week, usually in long sessions where I'd watch videos and work through materials visually since that's how I learn best. I didn't always make it to live classes because of my work schedule and other commitments, but I made sure to catch up through the recorded content. I'd often study late at night when my house was quiet and I could focus without distractions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, logging in every day, but other weeks everything would pile up at once and I'd have to cram. With 17 online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about when I gave each one attention. Deadlines were really the main driver of when I'd focus on specific courses.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or wanting to check if there were new materials posted. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more regularly because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or points. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post and that would pull me in. Fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me.
Q4: Yeah, the gamified elements really helped make logging in feel more routine. Once I started seeing my progress tracked visually and competing a bit with myself, it became almost addictive to check in. I'd log in about 8 times a week just to see where I stood and what I could complete next. It turned from a chore into something that felt more like a game, which helped a lot with my low motivation.
Q5: The video content was absolutely essential for me - I watched over two hours' worth regularly because I'm a visual learner and that's how concepts really click for me. The gamified tasks were also super useful because they broke things down into manageable pieces and gave me that sense of accomplishment. Discussion boards helped too because seeing how peers approached problems gave me different perspectives I wouldn't have thought of on my own.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the dense reading materials, especially long PDFs without any visual elements. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or didn't have points attached, it was easy to push aside. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from videos. With limited resources at home, I had to be selective about what I spent my time and energy on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to my grades or when it had that gamified element that made progress visible. If I could complete something and immediately see a result - like points earned or a badge - that felt rewarding. Activities that helped me understand concepts visually or that I knew would show up on exams were priorities. The sense of accomplishment from completing tasks kept me going even when motivation was low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to spend time on, honestly. Required ones had this weight of necessity that pushed through my low motivation, while optional ones needed to really grab my interest or offer clear value. When the gamified system made optional activities count toward something visible, they felt more worthwhile. But when I was stressed and scared about keeping up, optional stuff was the first to go.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs to study when my internet was unreliable or when I wanted to work somewhere without wifi. I kept handwritten notes and diagrams because drawing things out helps me remember them better. I also used free YouTube tutorials and other external resources when I needed concepts explained differently than the course materials presented them.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper that the LMS would never capture. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times, pausing and rewinding to really understand things, but that wouldn't show as new activity. Group study sessions with classmates where we'd work through concepts together happened completely outside the system. A lot of my real learning happened in those invisible moments.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach for videos especially. I'd access them once on the LMS, download them, and then watch them multiple times offline on my phone or laptop. This was partly because of my internet situation and partly because I learn better when I can pause and rewind freely without worrying about buffering. The LMS would show one view, but I might have watched something five or six times.
Q12: There were definitely periods when fear and stress made it harder to engage. When multiple deadlines converged across my 17 courses, I'd feel paralyzed and scared about falling behind. My low resilience meant that setbacks hit me hard and it took time to recover mentally. Sometimes personal stuff or work obligations would pile up and the course would temporarily take a backseat, even though I knew I shouldn't let it.
Q13: Definitely. The stress was constant and sometimes overwhelming, which affected how much mental energy I had for the LMS. When I was exhausted from work or other responsibilities, I'd still log in but my engagement was more superficial. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules until I absolutely had to face them. With limited resources, I also had to balance study time against other necessities.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to really absorb anything. I'd scroll through materials without really processing them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open resources intending to study but end up just staring at the screen feeling overwhelmed. The activity would show up in the logs but the learning wasn't really happening in those moments.
Q15: I definitely postponed things that felt too difficult or that triggered my anxiety about not understanding. Some assignments I'd avoid until the last possible moment because starting them made me confront how much I didn't know. Even knowing they were important, the fear of failure sometimes kept me from beginning. The gamified elements helped break this pattern by making tasks feel less intimidating and more like achievable challenges.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system since my internal motivation was pretty low. I'd see a deadline approaching and that fear of failure would kick in, pushing me to engage intensely with the LMS. My activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably would have struggled to maintain any consistent engagement given everything else going on in my life.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood significantly and made me want to engage more - that post-intervention high feeling was real. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid the course for a bit while I processed it. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal and more like game progress. Peer interactions in discussions also provided informal feedback that kept me engaged.
Q18: The structure mattered a lot for someone like me with low motivation. When things were broken into clear, achievable chunks with visible progress tracking, I could push through. The gamified elements gave structure that my own self-discipline couldn't always provide. However, when the course structure was unclear or overwhelming, my attendance and engagement would drop. I needed that external scaffolding to stay on track.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement much easier because they turned studying into something with immediate, visible rewards. Visual content was accessible for my learning style, which helped a lot. However, when materials were text-heavy or when the path forward wasn't clear, engagement became harder. The discussion board format was good for connecting with peers, but sometimes the sheer volume of 17 courses made it hard to keep up with everything.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand but struggling, so they keep accessing materials without feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety or fear like I sometimes did - engaging with content but freezing when it comes to actually producing something. It could also be someone who learns better offline and uses the LMS mainly to access materials they'll study elsewhere.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was pretty low but I still passed because I was doing a lot of work offline that didn't show up in the system. I'd download materials and study them extensively on my own time. Someone might have figured out efficient ways to learn that don't require constant LMS presence. The logs don't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone's most active, which resources they access, and how they respond to deadlines. They can track completion rates and participation in discussions. But they miss the depth of understanding, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the activity, and whether someone is truly engaged or just going through motions. They can't see fear, struggle, or those breakthrough moments of understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and actively thinking about concepts, making connections, and wanting to learn more. For me, it also means overcoming that scared feeling and pushing through low motivation to actually participate. True engagement is when the learning feels meaningful, not just like checking boxes - though sometimes the gamified boxes helped me get there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt, or how much I struggled with motivation despite maintaining decent activity levels. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline study, the peer support that happened outside the platform, or how the gamified interventions actually shifted my emotional state from anxious to accomplished. It can't see that behind my passing grade was someone constantly fighting against limited resources and high stress to keep going.
